items.title: How a weight loss drug might give Denmark leverage in Trumps bid for Greenland | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The President of the European Council emphasized that Denmark's territorial integrity is a "crucial issue" for the European Union. This statement came in response to repeated comments from US President Donald Trump, who has expressed interest in acquiring Greenland and has not ruled out using force. Greenland, an autonomous territory within the Kingdom of Denmark, is at the center of this dispute.
Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen received support from European allies during visits to Berlin and Paris this week. She has consistently stated that the island is not for sale. A recent survey shows that 85% of Greenlanders oppose becoming part of the United States.

items.title: German parliament debates banning AfD party ahead of elections | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The German parliament has been discussing proposals to prohibit the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party.
Germany's domestic intelligence agency is monitoring the AfD due to concerns that it may pose a threat to democracy. While some factions within the party have been officially designated as far-right extremist, the AfD is expected to perform well in next month’s national elections. Opinion polls suggest it could emerge as the second-largest party in parliament. However, other parties have stated they will not enter a coalition with the AfD to form a government.

items.title: Trump and Putin: Will they make a deal at Ukraine’s expense? | To the point
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Russia has been waging a war of aggression against Ukraine for almost three years. Now, possible negotiations between U.S. President Donald Trump and Russia’s Vladimir Putin are raising the prospects of a peace deal. But would it be one that’s fair to Ukraine? Trump says he wants to meet Putin as soon as possible to find an end to the war. Putin, for his part, says he’s ready to negotiate – How seriously to take that? Well, Russian troops are still advancing in eastern Ukraine – and the question of how long the Ukrainians can hold out is becoming increasingly urgent. President Volodymyr Zelensky is demanding security guarantees for Ukraine. The topic: Trump and Putin: Will they make a deal at Ukraine’s expense?

Our guests: 
Gesine Dornblüth, Journalist
Vladimir Esipov, DW
Jessica Berlin, CEPA

00:00 Opener

02:08 The change of leadership in Washington could mean a new dynamic

03:00 Ukraine has a manpower problem

03:40 Video: Situation on the frontline

05:15 Is Ukraine fighting a losing battle?

08:05 Any victory for Putin is a loss for Trump

10:00 Video: Trump and Putin: A starting point for peace talks?

12:10 What kind of security guarantees for Ukraine?

13:30 European troops at the border between Russia and Ukraine?

15:00 Republicans know that Russia is the enemy

16:40 Is Putin really ready to talk?

19:40 Russia’s aims haven’t changed: Destroying Ukraine

23:50 What could the West offer Russia?

24:30 Does Russia have to pay for all the damage it has done?

25:20 This war won’t end on the US or Russia’s terms

items.title: DRC: UN peacekeepers die in clashes with M23 rebels | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>M23 fighters moved into Goma this week. They've reportedly taken control of several districts of the city and have been involved in running battles with Congolese forces. The fighting has forced thousands of civilians to flee across the border to neighboring Rwanda - who the Congolese government and the UN accuse of backing the rebel insurgency. At least 17 peacekeepers from a southern African regional force and the UN's DRC mission have been killed in the fighting. The M23's rapid advance on Goma has seen it take several key towns in the mineral-rich region in the country's east.

items.title: Will China's DeepSeek burst the American AI bubble? | DW News Desk
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Chinese artificial intelligence company DeepSeek and its latest open-source large language model (LLM) are sending shockwaves through the world's tech industry. DeepSeek's LLM appears to match or even beat its competitors at a fraction of the cost. And DeepSeek is by far not the only AI model coming out of China. Alibaba's Qwen and Bytedance's Doubao are other examples of competitors catching up with American companies like OpenAI. US President Donald Trump says it's a 'wake-up call' for Silicon Valley, but how real is DeepSeek's AI revolution?  Can this Chinese startup truly overtake the US tech giants spending billions to win the AI race?

Today's panel:

items.title: How are returning Syrians being received by the people that stayed in the country? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>EU foreign ministers meeting in Brussels have agreed on a plan to ease sanctions on Syria following the ousting of the Assad regime.

The bloc's foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said the plan would be swiftly implemented to help kickstart Syria's recovery after almost 14 years of civil war. But she said it could be reversed if the new government in Damascus does not respect human rights. Current sanctions include a ban on Syrian oil exports and financial restrictions. 

During Assad's regime, millions of Syrians left the country. Many of them are returning home for the first time in a decade. DW colleague Rama Jarmakani is one of them.

items.title: How one Holocaust survivor decided to fight extremism | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The name Auschwitz has become synonymous with death, suffering and inhumanity. At least 1.1 million people were murdered there. More than 900,000 of them were Jews. 
Eighty years ago, on January 27, 1945, soldiers of the Red Army liberated Auschwitz. That day is recognized in Germany as a nationwide day of remembrance for the victims of National Socialism.  
Helene Joffe grew up in a Jewish family in France and went into hiding to escape the Nazi death camps in 1943 when she was just 8 years old. She vividly remembers the impact the Holocaust had on her family. After the war she moved to South Africa and started a new life as a teacher. DW met the 90-year-old survivor.

Chapters:
0:00 80th anniversary of Auschwitz liberation
7:50 Stephan Lehnstaedt, Holocaust and Jewish Studies Professor
13:01 Katrin Steffen, University of Sussex

items.title: 80th anniversary of Auschwitz liberation marked by survivors and world leaders | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
World leaders and Holocaust survivors gathered in the Polish town of Oswiecim to mark the 80th anniversary of the liberation of the Nazi death camp Auschwitz-Birkenau. 
The ceremony focussed on testimonies of surviving former inmates. Some wore blue and white scarves, the colors worn by the death camp prisoners. 
Several international leaders attended the event including German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, French President Emmanuel Macron and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy. 
Auschwitz was the largest of the extermination camps and has become a symbol of Nazi Germany's genocide of six million Jews. Around 1.1 million people, the majority Jewish, were murdered at the site in German-occupied Poland between 1940 and 1945.

items.content:

items.title: M23 rebels in DR Congo say they have captured Goma | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
M23 rebels in the Democratic Republic of Congo say they have captured the eastern city of Goma, after days of heavy fighting with government forces and UN peacekeepers. 
The M23 militia is backed by neighboring Rwanda, and the United Nations has warned of a major humanitarian crisis. Thousands of civilians have already fled across the border into Rwanda. There are reports that troops from the two neighbouring countries have been firing at each other across the border. Goma is the biggest city in eastern Congo - home to around 1.7 million residents and displaced people.

items.title: Israel lets Palestinians return to northern Gaza: What will they find? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Thousands of displaced Palestinians are now crossing into northern Gaza for the first time in over a year. Israel has agreed to allow their passage as part of the ceasefire deal with the militant Hamas group. 
Gazans began returning on Monday morning after waiting days to cross. Their return had been delayed after Israel blocked their passage, accusing Hamas of breaching the terms of the truce by not freeing civilian hostages first. The militants have agreed to release more Israeli captives this week in exchange.

items.author: DW News
items.content:

items.title: HIV breakthrough drug priced out of reach for those who need it most | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The UN agency leading the world's response to HIV and AIDS, is making an urgent call to action: UNAIDS wants a groundbreaking HIV prevention drug to be made available to everyone who needs it. The drug, Lenacapavir, requires just two injections a year and has shown remarkable success in trials. 
But there's a problem: it currently costs each patient more than forty thousand dollars per year - that's far too expensive for most.

items.title: Has Lukashenko silenced the Belarusian opposition? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Exit polls in Belarus indicate that longtime ruler Alexander Lukashenko has won the country's presidential election. As polls close there, election authorities are already reporting that Lukashenko has captured 87-point-6 percent of the vote. Lukashenko - who has close ties to Moscow -  has now been in power since 1994. Election observers say the vote was largely orchestrated - rigged - and that any meaningful opponents are in prison or exiled.

items.title: How Arab countries are responding to Trump's 'clean out' Gaza remarks to expel Palestinians
items.author: DW News
items.content:
On Saturday, US President Trump suggested that Palestinians be expelled from the Gaza Strip to "clean out" the territory. Trump called the Gaza Strip a "demolition site" and that ethnically cleansing Palestinians from the enclave could be a temporary or a long-term arrangement. President Trump said he had spoken to Jordanian King Abdullah about Jordan taking in Palestinians, and that he would speak to Egyptian President el-Sissi on Sunday for Egypt to do the same. Trump's "clean out" remarks have stirred controversy, as any forced expulsion of Palestinians would violate international law and jeopardize the creation of a Palestinian state.

items.title: Palestinian Islamic Jihad announces release of Israeli civilian prior to next hostage-prisoner swap
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The militant group Palestinian Islamic Jihad in Gaza says it will free Israeli civilian hostage Arbel Yehud before the next planned swap where Israel is to release Palestinian prisoners and detainees in exchange for Hamas returning Israeli hostages. According to the terms of the Israel-Hamas ceasefire agreement, Arbel Yehud should have been handed over by Hamas this past Saturday. Hamas, however, delayed the release over what they called technical reasons. Israel has blocked tens of thousands of Palestinians from returning to their homes in northern Gaza, and says their return is conditional on Yehud's release.

DW speaks to journalist Balig Sladeen.

items.title: Israeli forces remain in southern Lebanon past withdrawal date, kill at least 22 Lebanese | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Tensions have erupted along the Lebanon-Israel border as Israeli forces remain in southern Lebanon past the agreed withdrawal deadline under a fragile ceasefire with Hezbollah. According to Lebanese health officials, at least 22 people were killed and over 80 injured when Israeli troops fired on residents attempting to return to their homes.  The deadly incident is likely to enflame the humanitarian crisis for the 112,000 displaced Lebanese.

items.title: Can the EU compete with China’s rail plans? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
A deadly accident at a train station in Serbia has put a spotlight on China’s outsized role in the Balkans and Hungary, particularly its funding for large rail projects. Europe is now trying to keep up, but is it enough? DW looks at a geopolitical competition centered around rail, trade and the future orientation of a region on the EU’s periphery.

items.title: Israeli troops kill civilians returning to southern Lebanon villages | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The Lebanese Health Ministry says at least two people are dead in southern Lebanon after Israeli soldiers fired on residents carrying Hezbollah flags. 
The ministry said another 31 people had been injured near the border with the Israeli-occupied Golan Heights. Tensions have been rising in the area as residents have been returning to their homes despite the presence of Israeli forces. The troops were set to withdraw by Sunday under a ceasefire agreement between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah militants. But Israel said on Friday they would remain there longer.

items.title: Can Yunus master the transition in Bangladesh? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Will sectarian tensions overwhelm the new government of Bangladesh—Islamist parties repressed under the former regime now can pursue their ideology. Is the country's Hindu minority being targeted as some in India claim? Does the change of government open the door for a thaw in relations with Pakistan for the first time since indepdence and how would that be seen in New Delhi? 
  
Since former Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina fled Bangladesh in August 2024 the country’s interim leader, Nobel laureate Muhammad Yunus has been confronted with mulple challenges. Hasina fled to India after 15 years in power and faces multiple investigations including for potential crimes against humanity.  

Is Bangladesh on the brink of chaos or can it master the transition to stability and democracy? This is the fourth episode of DW’s Inside Asian Conflicts series.

items.author: DW News
items.content:

The Republic of Ireland recorded wind gusts of 183 kilometres an hour -- its strongest on record. Eowyn left almost one million households and businesses in Ireland without power. Scotland and the North of England are also taking a battering from the storm, with rail services cancelled and schools closed out of safety concerns.

items.title: Can Friedrich Merz win over female voters? | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Michaela Küfner, DW Chief Political Editor:
He himself recognises that the CDU for success needs women voters. But the younger the women are that get polled, the worse he does. Below 18, he's even below 9%. Well, you can say that doesn't really matter because they're not going to vote anyway. The voting age in Germany is 18, but above that it's not that terribly much more. And he just simply comes across as a man of the 90s. He's tone deaf to the issue of women's representation. Just as an example, in 2023, there were seven tickets for the Munich Security Conference for his party. He sent seven men. He doesn't have anyone who's very a close advisor to him who's female, his spokespeople, all men. Only the head of the Konrad Adenauer house of the party, that's a female speaker.

items.title: Who is Friedrich Merz, Germany's likely next leader? | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
John Kampfner, Journalist and Author:
He's a man who, as you say, he's been in politics for a long time. He's acerbic. He doesn't suffer fools gladly. He's capable of being quite rude to people. He knows his mind. Both because of his relationship to Angela Merkel and feeling that she had stabbed him in the front and the back, and is very keen to do the same now to her during this election campaign and afterwards. He has committed what, for some traditional Germans think is a cardinal sin and worked in the private sector for the asset company Blackrock and sort of a big global company, which in the sort of millennial feeling of Germany's centre-left, is regarded as beyond the pale.

items.title: How Germany's super-rich stay super-rich | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The number of billionaires is rising rapidly worldwide, including in Germany. Despite an inheritance tax, wealth continues to grow untaxed for the ultra-wealthy. How is that possible? 

Ahead of the elections in Germany, most political parties overall promise tax relief in their election manifestos. While the Greens, SPD and BSW propose reforms to the inheritance tax aimed at generating an additional €3 billion annually, other parties advocate for reductions. The AfD's proposal could even slash annual inheritance tax revenue in Germany by as much as €10 billion. These policies could widen the gap between the rich and the poor.

items.title: Friedrich Merz: What to expect from Germany’s likely next leader | Berlin Briefing
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Friedrich Merz is the frontrunner to become Germany’s next leader after February’s elections. If he succeeds, he’ll face huge challenges: restoring economic growth after two years of recession, and delivering political stability after the infighting of the outgoing coalition. Experts warn that the stakes for a Merz-led government will be high: failure to deliver could fuel the far right. In this edition of Berlin Briefing, DW’s Richard Walker and Michaela Küfner break it all down with writer John Kampfner. Who is Friedrich Merz? Why did he have a feud with Angela Merkel? How would he deal with Donald Trump, Vladimir Putin, the Ukraine war and the challenge of China? And can he deliver the reboot that he says Germany needs?

items.title: Belarus elections expected to extend Lukashenko's 31-year reign | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Belarus is holding elections this weekend, which are expected to deliver a seventh term in power to President Alexander Lukaschenko. He has already ruled the eastern European country for 31 years. The opposition has called the vote a farce, while the European Parliament is warning of a sham election. The country's last presidential vote sparked nationwide protests following allegations of vote rigging. The government responded with a brutal crackdown, which led many critics to flee Belarus. Some are continuing their activism from abroad -- like from neighboring Poland. DW correspondent Sergei Satanovskii reports from the Polish capital Warsaw.

items.title: DRC conflict: M23 rebels capture vast territory, close in on regional capital city | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Thousands of people are on the move in the east of the Democratic Republic of Congo, fleeing fresh fighting in the region. The M23 rebel group has captured swathes of territory from the Congolese Army in one of Sub-Saharan Africa's most mineral-rich regions. M23, which is backed by Rwanda, is now closing in on the city of Goma where many people have fled to. The ongoing violence has created one of the world’s largest and most complex humanitarian crises. The UN says more than 400,000 people have been displaced this year alone.

items.title: How Trump’s tough leadership could bring peace to the Middle East | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Aidarous al-Zubaidi, vice president of Yemen’s Presidential Leadership Council, praised the return of President Donald Trump as key to curb the Iran-backed Houthis. In an interview with DW’s Ben Fajzullin at the World Economic Forum in Switzerland, he called for a stronger U.S.-led assault on the rebel group.  
The Houthis have been attacking Israel, as well as commercial Western vessels in the Red Sea, in what they said was solidarity with Palestinians in the war between Israel and Hamas in Gaza. The Houthis control the northwest of Yemen. The rest is held by the Southern Transitional Council, headed by Zubaidi. The armed group wants an independent southern Yemen.

items.title: ICC: Taliban behind crimes against women | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The International Criminal Court's chief prosecutor Karim Khan has announced he is seeking arrest warrants aganist senior Taliban leaders in Afghanistan over the persecution of women. Since the Afghan Taliban took control of Kabul in 2021, they have issued dozens of decrees that restrict women from public life and education. ICC chief Khan has said that Afghan women and girls, as well as the LGBTQ community, were ''facing an unprecedented, unconscionable and ongoing persecution by the Taliban.''

items.title: Live: WEF roundtable on the global economy | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Nearly five years since the COVID-19 pandemic upended the global economy, growth is slow but stable, inflation has gradually declined in advanced economies and trade trends have turned positive. Despite this, there remain challenges such as high public debt burdens, ongoing geo-economic tensions and the potential impact of industrial policies on smaller countries. Given this landscape, what are the plausible scenarios for the global economy in 2025?

Speakers:
Christine Lagarde, President, European Central Bank
Kristalina Georgieva, Managing Director, International Monetary Fund (IMF)
Laurence D. Fink, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, BlackRock

items.title: Economy and tariffs under President Trump: Who wins and who loses? | To the Point
items.author: DW News
items.content:
From the Panama Canal to Mars – Donald Trump is thinking big at the start of his second term as US president. And has experts guessing about his economic policies and what they’ll mean for the rest of the world.

The US economy is already booming after the Biden administration and has outpaced many competitors in terms of technology, productivity and economic growth.

Trump wants to extend this lead even further – with billions for AI projects, oil drilling, and with tax cuts – but also with controversial trade tariffs. Who will profit from his drastic “America First” policy? And who will it harm?

Melissa Eddy is a correspondent with The New York Times in Berlin, covering Germany’s economy, businesses and social issues.

Felix Lee is a geoeconomics editor at Germany´s Süddeutsche Zeitung Dossier here in Berlin.

And Jürgen Matthes is the Head of the Cluster for International Economic Policy, Financial and Real Estate Markets at the German Economic Institute, joining us from the city of Cologne.

Chapters:
00:00 Opening
01:50 Trump’s “America First” and “Whatever it takes” policy
02:50 Markets are cheering Trump’s second term
03:10 The US economy is burdened with debt
03:30 Trump aims to push back against globalization
04:00 Trump as a deal maker
04:20 Trump’s supporters are ideologists and well-prepared
05:00 Europe isn’t ready for a second Trump presidency
05:55 The US economy is growing steadily
07:20 A great deal of disruption lies ahead
07:40 Topic introduction video: America’s “Golden Age”
09:05 Americans are concerned about rising prices
10:20 Is Trump undermining his own country?
11:10 Trump will roll back some policies
11:40 Europe will have to rely on gas for a while
12:20 What will happen to oil prices?
12:48 Are the US already an oligarchy?
14:30 Do Americans trust big tech bosses?
15:20 Is Trump really a big businessman?
16:00 Topic introduction video: Mexico, Canada, Tariffs, and Jobs
17:50 Tariffs on China were considered a success
19:30 Is Europe prepared for tariff shock?
20:00 Europe only stands a chance if it stays united
21:10 The Chinese government is much more prepared than the Europeans
23:20 China will continue to depend on exports

items.title: German Defense Minister: We have a bigger responsibility for Europe's security than ever | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius spoke with DW's Chief Political Correspondent, Nina Haase, during his trip to Lithuania. In 2023, Pistorius signed a roadmap for the permanent deployment of a Bundeswehr brigade to the Baltic nation. The German brigade in Lithuania is set to be combat-ready by 2027.

The defense minister visited Lithuania during the first week of Donald Trump's second term as US president. When asked if this visit was part of a strategy to demonstrate Europe’s proactive stance, Pistorius said that Europeans now have a bigger responsibility for Europe's security than ever before, regardless of any US administration.

The new legislation replaces references to "man" and "woman"  with "partner", allowing anyone to get married, as long as one is a Thai citizen. However the law will not apply to the children of same-sex partners, meaning many of them will remain at a disadvantage.

items.title: How the Trump Administration reshapes US politics from Day 1 | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
New policies and pardons from US President Donald Trump. The second Trump administration officially began on Monday and it has been 24 hours of non-stop executive orders coming from the White House - but not only from there.  Just hours after he was sworn in for a second term, Trump appeared on stage at a sporting arena in  Washington DC filled with his supporters.  That is where he signed his first series of executive orders in a piece of political theater merging a Trump rally with his presidential powers. He finished by throwing his pens into the crowd. Later, back in the Oval Office, he signed more orders. They included a sweeping pardon for around 1500 rioters who attacked the US Capitol four years ago, in an unsuccessful attempt to overturn Joe Biden's presidential election victory. A victory that Trump has always publicly refused to accept.

items.title: Israeli army chief Halevi resigns over Oct 7th failures as Gaza ceasefire holds | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The head of the Israeli military Herzi Halevi has announced that he will resign, citing security and intelligence failures that left Israel exposed to Hamas' surprise terror attack on October 7, 2023.

In a letter to Israel's Defense Minister, Israel Katz, Halevi said he would remain in the post until the completion of the Israel Defense Force (IDF)'s investigation into the October 7 attack before standing down on March 6.

Halevi's resignation comes as a fragile ceasefire between Israel and Hamas holds in the Gaza Strip.

Israel claims it has killed over 17,000 militants in the Gaza Strip, although it has not provided any evidence, while the Hamas-run health ministry in the enclave says over 47,000 Palestinians have been killed, the majority of whom they say were women and children.

Hamas fighters joined in celebrations over the weekend as the ceasefire began, in a public demonstration that the group still commands loyalty even diminished by 15 months of war.

Middle East expert H. A. Hellyer joined DW to discuss how Hamas, which is considered a terrorist organsiation by many countries, remains in control of Gaza.

00:00 Israeli army chief Halevi resigns
00:29 Hamas forces resurface after ceasefire
03:30 Middle East expert H.A. Hellyer explains how Hamas remains potent

items.title: Trump revokes Biden's sanctions on extremist Israeli settlers in occupied West Bank | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
US President Donald Trump has revoked sanctions put on extremist Jewish Israeli settlers over violence in the occupied West Bank.

His predecessor Joe Biden had placed sanctions on several settlers accused of attacking Palestinians in the West Bank back in February 2024. The measures froze any US assets and placed visa restrictions on those affected.

Violent attacks by settlers on Palestinians in the West Bank have increased massively since the terrorist attacks carried out by Hamas on October 7th, 2023.

Around 600,000 Israeli settlers live in settlements across the West Bank that are considered illegal under international law.

DW spoke to London School of Economics professor and author Fawez Gerges about Trump's move and his administration's approach to the settler movement.

items.title: Trump pushes anti-migrant policies as he returns to power | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
After his inauguration, President Donald Trump implemented a series of anti-immigration policies, including declaring an emergency at the US-Mexico border. He also moved to end birthright citizenship, which was enshrined in the Constitution after the Civil War. The details are still unclear about how many troops might be deployed to the region, and how the new administration will use the military to support Trump's promised crackdown on illegal immigration. Some of his plans are likely to face significant legal hurdles, immigration advocacy groups have already announced they will challenge Trump's plans. But for now, millions of migrants, facing dire situations in their home countries, are left in despair.

items.title: What a deal between the Trump administration, Russia, and Ukraine could look like | DW News Desk
items.author: DW News
items.content:
US president Donald Trump has presented himself as a peacemaker: “My proudest legacy will be that of a peacemaker and unifier. That’s what I want to be — a peacemaker and a unifier,” he said in his inaugural address. He vowed to end the Ukraine war immediately upon taking office, raising expectations that he would leverage aid to force Kyiv into making concessions to Russia, which invaded in February 2022.
 
However, in unusually sharp remarks about Russian President Vladimir Putin, whom he has previously admired, Trump pointed to Russia’s struggling economy and heavy battlefield losses. “He should make a deal. I think he’s destroying Russia by not making a deal,” Trump told reporters upon his return to the Oval Office.
 
Trump also mentioned that he was preparing to meet with Putin. A summit between the two during his first term gained notoriety after Trump appeared to accept the Russian leader’s word over that of U.S. intelligence.

items.title: President Trump signs first round of executive orders, withdraws from Paris agreement | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Donald Trump is once again president of the United States. He took the oath of office and began his second term as the 47th president of the United States, promising what he called a 'new golden age for America.' At a rally of his supporters packed inside a Washington DC stadium, Trump wasted no time and began signing a series of executive orders.  One of the first was to withdraw the United States from the Paris climate treaty. 

For some analysis of Trump's first moves in office and their implications we spoke to DW correspondent Max Zander in Washington D.C., Brian Murray, Director of the Nicholas Institute for Energy, Environment & Sustainability at Duke University, and Ethan Bearman, an attorney and adjunct professor at Loyola Law School Los Angeles.

items.title: What the world can expect in Trump's second term | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Donald Trump is just hours away from being sworn-in as the next president of the United States. On the eve of his inauguration, he held a campaign-style rally in Washington - where he repeated his pledge to begin mass deportations of undocumented migrants as soon as he's back in power. Shortly before the rally, he participated in a wreath-laying ceremony at Arlington National Cemetery honouring fallen members of the US military.
The effects of a second Trump presidency extend across the globe and very much here in Germany. His planned tariffs on European goods will impact the German economy, and his stance on NATO members' defense spending will impact German security. As Germany prepares to go to the polls on the 23rd of February, the Trump effect is front of mind among German leaders.

items.title: How Day 1 of the Israeli-Hamas ceasefire unfolded | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Israel has freed 90 Palestinian prisoners as part of its ceasefire deal with Hamas. Early on Monday morning, several buses carrying the detainees left the gates of Israel's Ofer prison, just outside the West Bank city of Ramallah. Hundreds of people gathered to celebrate the release. The freed prisoners are said to be women and teenage boys from the West Bank and Jerusalem. They are the first of 1900 Palestinians Israel is set to release in exchange for 33 Israeli hostages in phase one of the deal.
Earlier, the first Israeli hostages were freed by Hamas militants as part of the deal.  Three women held captive for 471 days were finally brought back home to Israel - alive.  For other families, the wait continues. The six-week pause in fighting, which began on Sunday, will also allow hundreds of truckloads of aid per day to be brought in to the devastated territory. With reporting from DW's Tania Krämer, and analysis from Middle East Analyst Giorgio Cafiero.

items.title: Can this ceasefire agreement be a first step toward lasting peace? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The first three Israeli hostages to be released by Hamas under the new Gaza ceasefire agreement have returned to Israel.
Emily Damari, Doron Steinbrecher and Romi Gonen were delivered to Israeli army officials by the International Red Cross after being handed over by Hamas in Gaza. The women have also been reunited with their closest family members. They're the first of 33 Israeli hostages set to be released during a six-week ceasefire now in effect in Gaza. Meanwhile, since the fighting paused early on Sunday, thousands of Gazans have been returning to what's left of their homes. Aid trucks have also been arriving in Gaza with desperately needed supplies, but the situation is dire. We spoke to Laura Blumenfeld, Middle East Analyst at the Johns Hopkins University in Washington D.C., and the Senior Coordinator for UNICEF, Hamish Young.

items.author: DW News
items.content:
A ceasefire between Israel and Hamas has begun in Gaza after a nearly three-hour delay. The truce didn't begin on schedule after Hamas failed to release the names of the three female hostages to be freed today on time. The Israeli government says that the first hostages are due to be released this afternoon. In return, 90 Palestinian prisoners will be freed at the same time. Aid agencies are set to use the six-week ceasefire to rush more support into the strip.

items.content:

But Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has warned the deal would not go ahead unless Hamas provided the names of the first Israeli hostages it will be releasing. He also stressed that the agreement is temporary -- and that Israel's military is ready to resume fighting if it deems it necessary. As people in the region anxiously await the start of the truce, and the delivery of much needed aid to Gaza, Israel has carried out more strikes. items.title: Sudanese army takes strategic town from RSF militia | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Reuters says the United States is imposing sanctions on Sudanese leader and army chief Abdel Fattah al-Burhan for his role in atrocities and refusal to participate in peace talks. The move comes after the Sudanese Army retook a key town from the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces. The RSF leadership confirmed that it had lost control of Wad Madani, a crossroad of key supply lines that link several states. The capital of el-Gezira state, the town is strategically situated south of Khartoum. Analysts say its recapture could mark a significant turning point in the war, which the army and the RSF have been fighting since April 2023. The return of the army triggered scenes of joy across the country, and in the retaken town.

items.title: Will banning TikTok resolve social media issues of data privacy and perceived foreign influence?
items.author: DW News
items.content:
TikTok said it would "go dark" in the United States on Sunday unless the government intervenes and assures that service providers won't be held liable for breaking a law banning the video-sharing platform. The statement comes after the US Supreme Court upheld a law banning TikTok from operating in America, unless the platform is sold by its Chinese parent company. The justices agreed the risk to national security from TikTok's Chinese ownership trumps concerns about limiting freedom of speech. TikTok has around 170 million users in the United States. President-elect Donald Trump says he will review the Supreme Court ruling and decide on TikTok's future.

items.title: Who will be in charge of rebuilding Gaza? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Following a meeting that went into the early hours of Saturday morning, the Israeli Cabinet approved the terms of a six-week Gaza ceasefire and hostage release deal. The Office of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu announced that the Israeli government had "approved the framework for the return of the hostages." The agreement is set to take effect on Sunday and be implemented in three stages. Phase one would see Israeli troops withdraw from densely populated areas of Gaza. Hamas would release 33 hostages, while Israel would free hundreds of Palestinian prisoners. Aid agencies are also preparing a relief operation to ease Gaza's humanitarian crisis. If successful, phase two would oversee the withdrawal of Israeli troops from the Gaza Strip following the return of all living hostages, and phase three would involve a lengthy rebuilding process of Gaza’s decimated infrastructure.

items.title: Beyond a ceasefire deal: What are the roadblocks to a two-state solution? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The ceasefire in Gaza would release dozens of hostages held there and pause the 15-month long Israel-Hamas war, bringing the sides a step closer to ending their deadliest and most destructive fighting ever. The ceasefire deal itself is comprised of three stages, with the final stage requiring the reconstruction of Gaza. Yet the deal is not a blueprint for establishing a two-state solution. DW speaks to Nomi Bar-Yaacov, international lawyer and arbitrator, who discusses what’s necessary for a lasting peace between Israelis and Palestinians. Bar-Yaacov underscores the importance of both sides recognizing the other as human and argues that both the Israeli government and Hamas ought to be held accountable for the atrocities they have committed.

items.title: SpaceX 'Starship' explodes spectacularly while 'Super Heavy' booster makes successful return
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Airlines have been forced to divert flights over the Gulf of Mexico to avoid debris from a malfunctioning test of SpaceX's Starship rocket. The upper stage of the rocket broke up minutes after launching from Texas.

The Starship upper stage, two meters (6.56 feet) taller than previous versions, successfully detached from its Super Heavy booster around four minutes into flight as planned. But minutes later SpaceX Communications Manager Dan Huot said on a live stream that mission teams had "all communications with the ship. It was great to see a booster come down, but we are obviously bummed out about ship," Huot added, saying it would take time to determine what happened. The Starship was supposed to soar across the Gulf of Mexico from Texas on a looping trajectory around the planet. The spacecraft was packed with 10 mock satellites and was supposed to practice releasing them.

The upgraded Starship system stands roughly 37 stories tall and lifted off from the SpaceX facility in Boca Chica, Texas, at 5:38 p.m. local time (2238 GMT/UTC). It was the company's seventh test mission and the first test of 2025.

#SpaceX #Starship #SuperHeavyBooster

items.title: Cell phone captures debris falling from failed SpaceX rocket launch | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Cell phone footage captured the moment debris from a failed SpaceX rocket launch flew overhead,  as the wreckage left behind a colorful stream of smoke. A SpaceX Starship rocket failed in space eight minutes after launching from Texas, forcing airlines to alter their course to avoid falling debris and setting back Elon Musk's flagship rocket program.
The Starship upper stage successfully detached from its Super Heavy booster around four minutes into flight as planned. But minutes later SpaceX Communications Manager Dan Huot said on a live stream that mission teams had "all communications with the ship."
Cell phone footage captured the rocket's debris flying overhead, as the wreckage left behind a colorful stream of smoke.
Dozens of commercial flights diverted to other airports or altered course to avoid debris, according to flight tracking website FlightRadar24. Departures from some airports were also delayed.
The Starship was supposed to soar across the Gulf of Mexico from Texas on a looping trajectory around the planet. The spacecraft was packed with 10 mock satellites and had planned to practice releasing them. It was the company's seventh test mission and the first test of 2025.

items.title: Trump pressures Netanyahu to push ceasefire deal closer to finish line | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The outgoing US Secretary of State Antony Blinken has expressed confidence that a ceasefire between Israel and Hamas will go into effect on Sunday. Blinken said diplomats were in the process of tying up what he called a loose end. But Israeli national security minister Itamar Ben-Gvir has threatened to resign from the coalition government if Israel's cabinet approves the deal. The cabinet must agree to the deal before the cessation of hostilities can take effect. A cabinet vote on the deal was originally scheduled for Thursday, but has since been postponed.

items.title: Expansionist ambitions under Trump: A new era of land-grabs? | To the point
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Donald Trump is headed back to the White House, and already it appears it won’t be business-as-usual for US foreign policy.

What will “America First” mean for the world? Some hope he’ll succeed where his predecessor failed to find lasting peace in Ukraine and the Middle East.

But Trump's latest comments about annexing Greenland and Canada have cast doubt on the United States’ reliability as an ally.

What’s behind Trump’s apparent expansionist ambitions? 

Our guests:

Thorsten Benner, Co-founder and director of the Global Public Policy Institute in Berlin.

Moritz Gathmann, Foreign correspondent for the German weekly magazine, Stern.

Melinda Crane, American journalist

items.title: Can Europe survive or thrive in the new world order? | Business Beyond
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Europe's economic future is at stake. It has fallen far behind the US on multiple metrics and is not productive or innovative enough to close the gap. Now the return of Donald Trump to the White House has raised more existential questions. Can the EU do what is needed to secure a prosperous, democratic future for its citizens? In this episode of Business Beyond, we put the key questions to Eurogroup President Paschal Donohoe, former Italian prime minister Enrico Letta and Martin Wolf, the Financial Times chief economic commentator for almost 30 years.

For more from the experts we spoke to:
Mr Paschal Donohoe, Eurogroup President and Minister for Public Expenditure and Reform of Ireland - https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/eurogroup/president/biography/

items.title: 'Not in a bad position to rebuild' What does the ceasefire agreement mean for Hamas? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
After months of negotiations, Israel and Hamas have agreed on the terms of a ceasefire that could end the war in Gaza. Even though mediators have confirmed the deal, which is due to take effect on Sunday, Israel's government has yet to ratify it. It says final details are still being worked out. If the deal is implemented, the ceasefire should lead to the release of dozens of hostages held by Hamas, as well as hundreds of Palestinians detained in Israeli jails. That prospect has been welcomed by Palestinians and Israelis. But what does the ceasefire deal mean for the future of Hamas?

items.title: What does the Israel-Hamas ceasefire deal look like? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Qatar's Prime Minister has announced a ceasefire will take effect between Israel and Hamas on January 19. Qatar has been playing a key role as mediator in the ceasefire talks. In the US, both President Joe Biden and President-elect Donald Trump announced a deal has gone through. But Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu says final details still need to be worked out. 
Thousands were celebrating in in the streets in Gaza and Israel. The deal calls for a stop to the hostilities that started with the Hamas-led terror attack on October 7, 2023. Since then, tens of thousands of Palestinians, and hundreds of Israelis have been killed. In the first phase of the ceasefire deal, Hamas will hand over 33 hostages in exchange for hundreds of Palestinian prisoners.

items.title: 'Trump doesn't want Ukraine to lose' Ian Bremmer on Donald Trump's geopolitical approach | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Very soon, Donald Trump will be back in the White House as the US President. 
It's a change of power that's expected to have far-reaching consequences for the United States — and also for the rest of the world. Leaders from America's neighbors, here in Europe, and other capitals around the globe are waiting to see what the new US president will do once he returns to the Oval Office.
During Trump's first term in office, he snubbed some of America's traditional European allies, and pursued closer ties with autocrats like the North Korean leader Kim Jong Un. Trump also famously said he wanted a better relationship with Russia. Now, Vladimir Putin's Russia is at war with its democratic neighbor Ukraine, and both sides are pinning their hopes for the future on the new US administration.

0:00 What foreign policy changes will Donald Trump implement?

items.title: What capabilities do Russia and Ukraine have to attack deep into each other's territory? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Russia has once again launched dozens of long-range ballistic and cruise missiles, targeting Ukraine's national energy grid. Authorities said two critical infrastructure facilities were struck in the western region of Lviv, close to the Polish border. Emergency power cuts have been imposed in several regions. Kyiv also came under attack, with hundreds of residents taking shelter in underground metro stations across the capital. The attacks came just one day after Ukraine launched its largest-ever missile strikes on Russian infrastructure. An oil refinery, a fuel depot and an ammunition factory were among the targets hit, some of them around 1,000 kilometers inside Russian territory.

items.title: LIVE: Marco Rubio testifies at Senate confirmation hearing for US Secretary of State | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The US Senate Foreign Relations Committee holds a hearing to consider the nomination of Marc Rubio to become Secretary of State in the Trump administration. US President-elect Donald Trump's nominee for secretary of state Rubio is one of a trio of China hawks whom Donald Trump has tapped for senior roles, including Mike Waltz, who will be appointed Trump's national security adviser, and Elise Stefanik, nominated for ambassador to the United Nations. In 2018, Rubio co-sponsored the Reciprocal Access to Tibet Act, which seeks to deny entry into the United States to any Chinese officials who prohibit US citizens from entering Tibet, a territory claimed by China. He has also introduced legislation sanctioning China for its repression of the minority Uyghur population and the crackdown on protests in Hong Kong. The Chinese government responded by blacklisting Rubio.

items.title: Impeached South Korean president Yoon arrested after week-long standoff | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Suspended South Korean president Yoon Suk Yeol has been detained for briefly imposing martial law in December. The former prosecutor could face life in jail or even the death penalty if found guilty. The Asian country's anti-corruption agency confirmed Yoon had been detained hours after hundreds of investigators and police officers arrived at his presidential compound to apprehend him. South Korea entered its worst political crisis in decades after Yoon sent soldiers to storm the parliament following his declaration of martial law.

items.title: Fact check: Is Trump’s cabinet on track to becoming a misinformation machine? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Donald Trump's political rise to a second term in the White House was accompanied by claims that he spread misinformation on issues like immigration or public health.

Key figures in his team are accused of pushing false narratives. And analysts fear this could erode trust in political institutions. 

DW Fact Check dives deep into these claims and their potential impact. 

DW contacted the Trump administration for comment but received no answer until publication date.

01:51 Disinformation and Trump’s health secretary
03:03 Exaggerated number of unaccompanied migrants

05:43 A troubling trend?

items.title: Cautious optimism for a ceasefire as the death toll continues to mount in Gaza | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The terms of a deal between Israel and Hamas for a ceasefire in Gaza are close to agreement – that's according to US President Joe Biden.  

Negotiators are due to meet again later today in Doha to finalize a plan that would end the war as well as lead to the release of the hostages held in Gaza. Ceasefire talks have been taking place on and off for over a year and hopes for an agreement have been raised – and dashed – before. But now there is cautious optimism a breakthrough will happen this time.

A ceasefire agreement has eluded negotiators for months and DW's Benjamin Alvarez Gruber says there is skepticism in Washington that it will be achieved this time.

items.content:

items.title: German-Iranian activist released from Iran's notorious Evin prison | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Nahid Taghavi, a German-Iranian activist, has been released from Iranian custody after more than four years. 70-year-old Taghavi was arrested during a trip to Tehran in 2020. She was sentenced to ten years in jail for allegedly spreading propaganda against the state and for involvement in an illegal group. She claims to have been tortured and held in solitary confinement for months.

items.title: Mozambique swears in new parliament despite protests and the opposition's ceremony boycott | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
There have been sporadic protests in Mozambique's capital Maputo as the new parliament was sworn into office on Monday. Mozambique has experienced deadly unrest since last October's disputed election. The security forces have been accused of using excessive force against demonstrators, who say the vote was rigged. President-elect Daniel Chapo has called for calm ahead of his inauguration on Wednesday, and the opposition has called for a strike in protest.  Chapo's Frelimo party has been in power for fifty years. 

DW's Dianne Hawker reports on protests in Maputo.

items.title: German-Iranian human rights activist released from Iranian prison | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Nahid Taghavi, a German-Iranian activist, has been released from Iranian custody after more than four years. 70-year-old Taghavi was arrested during a trip to Tehran in 2020. She was sentenced to ten years in jail for allegedly spreading propaganda against the state and for involvement in an illegal group. She claims to have been tortured and held in solitary confinement for months. 

DW speaks to Daniela Sepehri, who together with Nahid Taghavi's daughter, helped campaign for Nahid's release.

items.title: How much would it cost to fix Germany's infrastructure? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Germany's trains are late, bridges are broken and the military crucially underfunded. With elections coming up in six weeks, how much would any new government have to spend to fix these issues?  
In their published election manifestos, all parties suggest tax cuts that would shrink government income overall. While CDU/CSU, FDP and AfD only propose tax cuts, the SPD and Greens propose small tax increases on wealth and inheritance. The parties are split on the constitutionally enshrined debt-brake. CDU/CSU, FDP and AfD want to adhere to the rule, while SPD and Greens have suggested changing the rule and debt-financing infrastructure spending.

items.title: Do Kurds on Syrian border have reason to fear Turkish annexation? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
It's been just over a month since Bashar al-Assad was toppled by Islamist-led rebel forces in Syria. Among the many groups hoping for greater representation in the new Syria is the country's Kurdish minority. During Syria's civil war, Syrian Democratic Forces - SDF - rebels fought to create a semi-autonomous area for the Kurdish population. 
About 10 percent of Syrians are ethnic Kurds. They're settled mostly in the north, along the border with Turkey, which has its own sizeable Kurdish minority.  The Syrian border city of Kobane is home to some 50,000 people, and local Kurds are increasingly fearful that Turkey could try to seize their city.

00:00 Kurds in Kobane fear a Turkish invasion
03:54 DW speaks with Hurcan Asli Aksoy, Head of the Centre for Applied Turkey Studies at the German Institute for International and Security Affairs

items.title: The Blue Line separating Israel and Lebanon | Mapped Out
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The Blue Line separating Israel and Lebanon was established in 2000 by the UN to reduce direct confrontations between Israeli forces and the Lebanese militia, Hezbollah. But ever since it was implemented, skirmishes have continued. And after Hamas attacked Israel from Gaza on October 7th, 2023, things escalated in Israel’s northern boundary. Why has this non-border between Israel and Lebanon never become a step towards peace in the Middle East instead of just another battleground? Will a ceasefire bring hope for a more peaceful future? And has the fall of Syria's long-term dictator, Bashar Assad, changed things on the ground?

items.title: Why did Manhattan Judge Merchan refuse to impose any punishment on Trump in hush money case?
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Manhattan Judge Juan M. Merchan sentences President-elect Trump to 'unconditional discharge' in New York hush money case. Judge Merchan spared Trump jail time or other fines. Critics argue that Judge Merchan could have, at the bare minimum, imposed a symbolic fine on Trump, whose status is now cemented as a felon. Legal commentators in favour of the ruling argue that any punishment could have impeded in Trump's upcoming official duties as president.

items.title: New data confirms 2024 is the first year to exceed 1.5°C above pre-industrial level | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Europe's climate monitor Copernicus confirmed that 2024 was the warmest year on record. A new report from the service shows last year's heat topped that of 2023, the previous record holder. 2024 was also the first calendar year more than 1.5 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels - the threshold scientists warn significantly increases the impacts of climate change. Scientists link the record heat to dozens of extreme weather events last year - devastating floods in Spain, crippling drought across Brazil and South America, and powerful typhoons and cyclones that battered parts of Asia. The extremes are forecast to continue into 2025 and scientists are urging immediate action to avert worst-case scenarios.

items.content:
in Germany, there's growing concern that Elon Musk is interfering in the campaign for next month's national elections. The billionaire has publicly endorsed the far-right Alternative for Germany party (AfD) and has called on Chancellor Olaf Scholz to stand down. On his social media platform X, Musk hosted a conversation with one of the AfD's leaders, Alice Weidel. She rejects comparisons of her party to the Nazis.

Authorities in Germany are investigating whether Thursday's talk on X constitutes an illegal party donation. Here's what a lobby watchdog had to say about the talk between Musk and Weidel: "The crucial point here is not that the talk itself is a donation. If that were the case, then a lot of media interviews would count as a donation. But that boosted reach on the platform X would constitute as a party donation, because usually X would sell this kind of boost, but with Elon Musk as the owner of the platform, his activities and this is proven or has been proven quite often, are artificially boosted. So the reach is much higher than for a user with a similar follower count that isn't the owner of X. And since this service of this boost in reach is supplied to the party Afd for free, this would constitute a party donation. And in Germany, party donations from entities from outside of the EU are illegal. And both Elon Musk and the Platform X are situated in the US. So this would be an illegal party donation."

If a court rules that this talk was an illegal party donation, what would that mean for Musk and the AfD?

#Germanelections #AliceWeidel #ElonMusk

items.title: Venezuela's opposition leader freed following months of being in hiding | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Venezuela's opposition leader has been freed, after she was reportedly detained leaving a protest against president Nicolas Maduro. 

Maria Corina Machado reappeared in Caracas on Thursday after being in hiding for months. Machado accuses Maduro of stealing a presidential election last July. Her team says she was knocked off her motorbike, detained and forced to record several videos before being released. Venezuela's opposition says there's evidence that Maduro lost the vote. He's set to be sworn in on Friday for his third six-year term as president.

items.title: What do Elon Musk and AfD leader Alice Weidel have in common? | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
X owner Elon Musk hosted a livestreamed talk with the co-leader and chancellor candidate of the Alternative for Germany party (AfD), Alice Weidel, on his social media platform on January 9. Although Musk billed Weidel as the ‘leading candidate to run Germany’, the AfD is polling second place ahead of early elections set for February 23, somewhere in the region of 20%, and is unlikely to get into government as all other parties have ruled out cooperating with a party that’s being investigated as far-right extremist.

In this episode of Berlin Briefing, DW’s Nina Haase and Michaela Küfner break down the content of the chat together with American journalist Kate Conger, co-author of “Character Limit – How Elon Musk destroyed Twitter”. From energy, immigration, and Israel, Ukraine and Nazi Germany, to free speech and Musk’s stated desire to colonize Mars – the conversation was peppered with misinformation and highly controversial statements (“Hitler was a communist”), and its many topics are analyzed by the team to build a picture of what was in it for the tech billionaire and for the co-leader of Germany’s far-right party.


Further information:

DW live blog on Musk + Weidel chat: https://www.dw.com/en/elon-musk-hosts-x-talk-with-german-far-right-afds-weidel/live-71245966

Kate Conger tour dates and more: https://linktr.ee/kateconger

More on the AfD’s foreign policy: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZFmpxuNxycM

DW Berlin Briefing episode on the AfD: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Evh-fCA8Tv4&list=PLT6yxVwBEbi22JF0-FgtDAMYcfTqL4V98&index=2

The FDP currently look likely to fall out of the German parliament entirely after February's elections, as they are polling below the five percent hurdle necessary to win seats.

items.title: Tensions rise as Mozambique's opposition leader Mondlane returns to the country | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Clashes over a disputed election in Mozambique have forced thousands to flee their homes, with many seeking refuge in neighboring Malawi. The unrest follows October's highly contested vote, which the opposition and also international observers claim was rigged. Venancio Mondlane, the main opposition leader, returned to Mozambique on Thursday after initially fleeing the country in the aftermath of the election. Mondlane has expressed his willingness to participate in talks to resolve the crisis. As the situation continues to unfold, many of those displaced are finding refuge in camps like Ten-gani in Malawi, where they face difficult conditions. DW’s reporter, Eldson Chagara, traveled there to speak with the displaced people about their journey and the ongoing struggles they now endure.

items.title: Is Trump more serious about taking Panama Canal and Greenland now than in first term? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
We start with US president-elect Donald Trump, now less than two weeks away from his inauguration, but already causing concern among America's allies.
During his latest press conference in Florida, he talked about expanding US territory in a way that would fundamentally redraw the map of the Western Hemisphere. 

He said he wanted to rename the "Gulf of Mexico" as the "Gulf of America". He once again talked about reclaiming the Panama Canal. And then the suggestions of making Canada the 51st US state... and getting Denmark to hand over Greenland.

Trump first floated the idea of buying the arctic island back in 2019 during his first term. It came to nothing then - but is more at play this time around? More after this report.

items.title: "Musk and Trump need each other" -- interview with Kara Swisher | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Kara Swisher, one of the most influential tech journalists in the U.S., told DW's bureau chief Ines Pohl that Elon Musk is supporting Germany's AfD to cause problems.  
"He is supporting this particular party to cause problems. He has issues with regulatory matters concerning European leaders, and he will do it over and over again across the world as he sees fit."  

"Any country that has the audacity to say that it wants to protect its consumers will be under siege. Europeans have been much more stringent in holding U.S. tech companies accountable. But that's why they're coming to attack you now." She says that the core interest of the tech billionaires is that they do not want any regulator to tell them what to do or what to sell in any way whatsoever.

Regarding the threats of the Trump administration towards journalists, she said: "We can't be cowed by these bullies. They certainly have a lot of power and influence on their side. But, you know, they've tried before; they can try again. We are going to have to fight them everywhere we can and understand that the independent press is critical to the operation of democracy. And there are people who think that."

Asked about whether Trump will be able to accept the popularity of Elon Musk and be willing to share the stage, she said: "I just don't know if either of them can afford to do that; they sort of need each other in terms of power and influence."

items.title: Will German conservatives' tough migration plans play into the far right's hands? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Germany's opposition Christian Democratic Union (CDU) is trying to woo voters with promises of tougher migration rules ahead of February's snap elections. 

But experts say that the conservative CDU, as well as its Bavarian sister party, the Christian Social Union (CSU), risk pushing yet more voters towards the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) by mirroring its policies. 

Opinion polls show that CDU leader Friedrich Merz will likely end up Germany's next chancellor after the country votes on February 23rd. 

Merz attended a meeting of the CSU where he promised that the Union bloc wouldn't repeat the 'mistakes' of the Merkel-era when it comes to migration. 

He also said he wanted to fix the severely underperforming German economy, and introduce tax reforms. He warned that Europe needs to be prepared for the 'disruptive' politics of US President-elect Donald Trump. 

DW's Chief Political Editor Michaela Küfner told us more about Merz's speech.

Maximilian Pichl, a professor of social law, from the RheinMain University of Applied Sciences, joined us to discuss why the CDU's plans might just fuel further support for the far-right.

items.title: Is Zuckerberg putting Meta at odds with EU regulations? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Experts fear Meta, the social networking giant that owns Facebook and Instagram, is risking opening the floodgates for misinformation by scrapping its independent fact-checking process.

Company founder Mark Zuckerberg said most content moderation will now done by the users, adopting a community-based model similar to that used by X.

The changes have currently only been announced for the company's platforms in the US, but European lawmakers in Brussels are watching the situation closely. DW's Jack Parrock joined us from the Belgian capital to explain more about how EU policymakers regard the plans.

The new policy marks a reversal of policies Mr Zuckerberg has long championed despite criticism from conservatives over alleged censorship. 

The Meta CEO acknowledged that the shift has been partly triggered by the reelection of Donald Trump. 

Matthias Kettemann, Professor of Innovation, Theory, and Philosophy of Law at Austria's Innsbruck University, said that the move was a clear attempt by Zuckerberg to ingratiate himself with Trump.

00:00 Meta fact checking changes spark concerns
02:50 DW's Jack Parrock discusses EU reaction to plans
07:58 Professor Matthias Kettemann criticizes fact check changes

items.title: What consequences will US genocide sanctions on Sudan's RSF militia have? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The US has imposed sanctions on the head of Sudan's Rapid Support Forces (RSF) militia, after accusing the group of committing genocide in Sudan's Darfur region.

Washington said the RSF had systematically murdered and raped members of certain ethnic groups in Darfur.

The Biden administration put sanctions on RSF leader Mohammad Hamdan Daglo, saying he bears responsibility for the actions of his fighters. 

The sanctions also target several businesses based in the United Arab Emirates, including one that handles gold likely smuggled out of Sudan.

The Arab country has faced repeated accusations of arming the RSF, which it has strenuously denied despite the evidence.

Tens of thousands have been killed in Sudan since the war between the country's army and the RSF broke out almost two years ago. 

Mutasim Ali from the Raoul Wallenberg Centre for Human Rights joined us from Kamapala, Uganda, to discuss the new sanctions.


00:00 RSF sanctioned over genocide accusations
00:30 Mutasim Ali from the Raoul Wallenberg Centre for Human Rights

items.title: Jean-Marie Le Pen dead: What legacy does father of the French far-right leave behind? DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Jean-Marie Le Pen, the firebrand co-founder of the French far-right party formerly known as the National Front, has died aged 96, French media reported on Tuesday.

Le Pen spent his later years locked in a bitter public feud with his own daughter, and three-time failed presidential candidate, Marine Le Pen.

She succeeded him as the head of the National Front in 2011, before booting him out of his own party over comments he made downplaying the Holocaust. 

The Elysee said President Emmanuel Macron sent his condolences to Le Pen's family.

In a brief statement, the Elysee added that "history will judge" his role in France's political life. 

Le Pen rocked France's political establishment when he unexpectedly reached the presidential election runoff vote against Jacques Chirac in 2002.

A polarizing figure in French politics, Le Pen gained popularity with his fiery rhetoric against immigration and multiculturalism that saw him frequently accused of racism. His extreme stances attracted both staunch support and widespread condemnation.

His statements, including Holocaust denial, led to multiple convictions and put his political alliances under strain.

#france #lepen #farright

items.author: DW News
items.content:
Three miners are feared dead as rescuers worked to reach nine men trapped inside a coal mine in India's Assam state. Authorities said the bodies had been spotted but teams were still trying to reach them.

The miners have been trapped since Monday, but rescue teams could only reach them on Tuesday as it is located in a remote part of the Umrangso area in the northeastern state's Dima Hasao district. 

They got trapped about 300 feet below ground level after water flooded in from a nearby disused mine. 

"The mine got flooded yesterday — the source was internal. They (the miners) probably hit some water channel and water came out and flooded it," a local official told Reuters.

The Indian army as well as the national and state disaster relief forces are trying to reach the trapped men.

items.title: Elon Musk goes after European leaders: Will they push back? | DW News Desk
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Musk’s incendiary interventions are a growing concern for governments in Europe. German Chancellor Olaf Scholz stated that he is staying “cool” despite critical personal comments made by Musk but expressed concern about the U.S. billionaire’s involvement in Germany’s election by endorsing the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party. 
 
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer on Monday condemned the “lies and misinformation” that he said are undermining U.K. democracy in response to a barrage of attacks on his government from Elon Musk. 
 
Recently, Musk has posted about a child sexual abuse case, particularly a series of incidents in northern England towns where groups of men, largely from Pakistani backgrounds, were tried for grooming and abusing dozens of girls. These cases have been used by far-right activists to link child abuse to immigration and accuse politicians of covering up the “grooming gangs” out of fear of appearing racist. 
 
Musk has called for a new public inquiry into cases of child abuse in northern Britain, accusing Starmer of failing to bring perpetrators to justice when he served as England’s director of public prosecutions between 2008 and 2013. Starmer defended his record, stating that he had reopened closed cases and “changed the whole prosecution approach” to child sexual exploitation. 
Elon Musk has also advocated for the release of Stephen Yaxley-Lennon, a far-right activist known as Tommy Robinson, who is currently serving a prison sentence for contempt of court. Starmer criticized this, saying, “People cheerleading Tommy Robinson are trying to get some vicarious thrill from the street violence that figures like Tommy Robinson promote.” 
 
Meanwhile, French President Emmanuel Macron framed Musk’s influence as a challenge to Europe’s democratic values, emphasizing the need for European unity and resilience against external disruptions. 
 
Without naming Musk directly, Macron referred to his alleged support for Germany’s far-right AfD party and his increasing interference in European elections. “Who could have imagined, 10 years ago, that the owner of one of the world’s largest social networks would intervene directly in elections, including in Germany?” Macron said. He warned of the risks posed by unchecked power in the hands of tech billionaires and the destabilizing impact they could have on democratic institutions.

items.author: DW News
items.content:

items.title: Death toll of Tibet earthquake rises to 95 as dozens of aftershocks hit the region | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
A powerful earthquake has killed at least 95 people in Tibet. Many others are trapped as dozens of aftershocks shook the region in the west of China and across the border in Nepal. 

Officials in the region said 130 people have been injured, and rescue workers are searching for people in the rubble. The quake's epicenter was about 75 kilometers northeast of Mount Everest, which straddles the China-Nepal border close to the Tibetan Buddhist holy city of Shigatse. Tremors were felt as far away as Kathmandu and India. The US Geological Survey measured the earthquake at magnitude 7.1 at a relatively shallow depth of about 10 kilometers.

00:45 Bhadra Sharma, Journalist

items.title: Why Musk's whims could stop him supporting Germany's far-right AfD party | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
We begin with the tech billionaire who's fast becoming a political force. Since taking charge of X -- formerly known as Twitter -- Elon Musk has used the social media platform as a platform for his own political views. And, after spending millions of dollars helping to secure Donald Trump’s return to the White House, he's now wading into European politics -- expressing support for far-right and anti-establishment parties across Europe, including here in Germany. In December, he tweeted his support for the far-right, Alternative for Germany party -- or AfD. With a general election in February, the AfD is polling second - and parts of the party are being monitored by German domestic security services as "possible threats to democracy". 

He's also attacked Germany's leaders, saying that Chancellor Olaf Scholz, "should resign immediately," and calling him an "incompetent fool". And of German president, Frank-Walter Steinmeier, he said, "Steinmeier is an anti-democratic tyrant! Shame on him".  Berlin has accused Elon Musk of election interference.

items.title: Why Volkswagen's unique history could cause its downfall | Business Beyond
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Volkswagen, Europe’s biggest car maker, is in crisis. A shrinking European market, more competition in China and slow electric vehicle uptake mean VW must cut costs. Israel's Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu says Hamas has not provided a list of names. But, a Hamas official earlier told Reuters news agency that the group had approved a list of 34 hostages presented by Israel to be exchanged in a possible ceasefire. Also earlier Israel said indirect negotiations had resumed in Qatar for a ceasefire and hostage release deal.

Despite the talks, Israel has stepped up airstrikes in Gaza, killing dozens of people.

00:00 Hostage and ceasefire deal?
02:09 Journalist Karim El-Gawhary

items.title: What makes far-right parties so appealing to voters? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Austrias President Alexander van der Bellen says he will hold talks with the leader of the far-right Freedom Party on Monday, to seek a way out of the country's political stalemate. 
The president made the announcement after meeting Chancellor Karl Nehammer - who is resigning following the breakdown of coalition talks between his conservatives and the Social Democrats. Together, the two parties had sought to prevent the far-right Freedom Party - which won the most votes in September's election - from forming a government. 
The breakdown of the talks could pave the way for far-right FPÖ leader Herbert Kickl to come to power, with the conservatives serving as junior partners - despite previous unwillingness to work with the far-right.

items.title: Why Canada thinks India is behind Sikh leader killing | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Ever since Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau accused Indian officials of orchestrating the murder of Hardeep Singh Nijjar on Canadian soil in 2023, Delhi has rejected the claim and demanded to see the evidence. Ottawa has denied a direct link to Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi or other cabinet members but said diplomats from the Indian embassy in Canada had gathered intelligence on Sikh separatists in the country. The allegations that Indian "agents" killed the Canadian citizen who was a leader in the Khalistan Sikh separatist movement led to a dramatic deterioration in relations between the two countries. Both sides have expelled top diplomats. 
In 2024 Canadian authorities indicted three Indian men for Nijjar’s murder. 

In this episode of DW's Inside Asian Conflicts we take a look at the the origins of Sikh secessionism and how it has impacted Indo-Canadian relations.

items.title: End of Russia-Ukraine gas deal: Will Germany fall back on Russian gas after the election? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The European Union says gas supplies are stable after deliveries of Russian gas via Ukraine ended on New Year's Day. Russian gas accounted for less than 10 percent of the European Union's gas imports in 2023, but some eastern members – including Slovakia and Hungary – were still heavily relying on them.

Just outside of the EU, the effects of the gas cuts were immediately felt in Moldova and especially its Russian-backed breakaway region of Transnistria, where heating and hot water supplies to households were cut off. Ukraine stopped the transit after a decade-long contract with Moscow expired. Kyiv said not renewing the deal was in the interests of national security, and a way to hurt Russia's economy. 

00:00 EU says supplies are stable after Ukraine cut Russian gas
00:50 Benjamin Schmitt, senior fellow at the University of Pennsylvania's Center for Energy Policy

items.title: French, German FMs meet Syria's new de facto leaders | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Germany's foreign minister Annalena Baerbock and her counterpart, France's Jean-Noel Barrot, are the first ministers from the EU to visit Syria and meet with de facto leader Ahmad al-Sharaa since rebels seized control of Damascus on December 8, 2024. Their visit to Syria is the highest-level western visit since the fall of the Assad regime. 

They expresseed support for the rebuilding of Syria. But they also made it clear that the EU will not finance Islamism - or tolerate discrimination against women or ethnic and religious groups.

DW talks to Christoph Schult in Damascus who is currently traveling with FM Baerbock. He is a journalist at Germany's Spiegel magazine.

There's some controversy over the Syrian leader's greeting for his EU visitors. Ahmad al-Sharaa shook hands with the French foreign minister Jean-Noel Barrot, a man, but not with Gemany's Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock, a woman. 

00:00 EU extends cautious olive branch to Syria
02:38 Christoph Schult in Damascus 
08:04 Anchal Vora, DW Correspondent in Brussels
