items.title: BSW: The new party disrupting German politics | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The “Bündnis Sahra Wagenknecht” party or BSW is scrambling definitions of left and right ahead of Germany’s upcoming elections. Its leader Sahra Wagenknecht stems from the far left, but now accuses her former allies of being obsessed with identity politics rather than the interests of the working class. Her party is running on a mix of policies with both left-wing and right-wing characteristics. 

In this episode of Berlin Briefing, DW’s Richard Walker and Matthew Moore break down the party’s positions together with German historian Katja Hoyer. From the BSW’s opposition to NATO, climate policies, and sending weapons to Ukraine, to its call for a stronger welfare state and a revival of Germany’s traditional industries with energy from Russia – the team build up a picture of this taboo-busting party and end with predictions about its impact on the political landscape.
_

items.title: Why does the FBI now say the New Orleans attacker acted alone? I DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The FBI says the man who commited the New Year's terror attack in New Orleans acted alone. 

Fourteen people were killed and dozens more injured when army veteran Shamsud Din-Jabbar rammed a truck into crowds of revellers in the city's French Quarter. The Texan is said to have pledged alliegance to the so-called Islamic State, and had made recordings in which he condemned music, drugs and alcohol. He was killed in a shoot-out with police after the attack. 

Meanwhile activities in New Orleans are resuming less than two days after the tragic event - and the famous street where it happened has been re-opened.

items.author: DW News
items.content:

At least 15 people were killed and dozens more injured when a man drove a truck at high speed into a crowd. The area remains an active crime scene. items.title: New Orleans attack: FBI investigating act of 'terrorism' | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Authorities in New Orleans say at least ten people have been killed and more than 35 injured as a man drove a pickup truck into crowds celebrating the New Year. 

The driver deliberately plowed into people at high speed in the city's famous Bourbon Street. The suspect was killed in a gun battle with police. The FBI says a suspected explosive device was found near the scene, and it's investigating the incident as an act of terrorism.

items.title: As Russia-Ukraine gas deal ends, who's the biggest loser? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Russia's gas transit to Europe via Ukraine has stopped as planned, after a key transit agreement expired at midnight. The pipeline was one of the last routes delivering Russian gas to Europe, supplying Slovakia and also Hungary -- two countries that have maintained close ties with Russia, despite its invasion of Ukraine. Kyiv had made it clear it would not renew the deal amid the war in the interests of national security.

items.title: Syria's new government should represent all groups in civil society | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syria has celebrated its first new year free of the Assad family's rule in five decades. Fireworks lit up one of the main squares in central Damascus as Syrians welcomed 2025 with high hopes for their future. The fall of the regime last month has allowed activists and ordinary people to speak openly for the first time about the country they want Syria to become. While some groups are connecting to unify their efforts, others are attempting to play down a quick transition to new governance. DW's Rama Jarmakani reports from the capital, Damascus. We spoke to  Anas Joudeh, founder and director of the Syrian group 'Nation Building Movement'.

#syria #syrianrebels #damascus

items.title: Georgia's ex-president Zourabichvili joins New Year protests | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
People in Georgia have also been ringing in the New Year - with all-night protests in the capital Tblisi. It was a scene of relative calm outside the parliament - following days of violence as security forces attacked demonstrators. The protests began at the end of November after the pro-Russian government halted talks on joining the European Union -- even though the vast majority of Georgians support membership. We spoke with DW's Anja Koch, who followed the protests and met two victims of the violence. A warning that some viewers may find this report upsetting.

items.title: How Poland's Donald Tusk could boost EU-US relations | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
With the start of January, Poland has assumed the rotating presidency of the Council of the European Union. This follows a turbulent term with Hungary at the helm, which saw Prime Minister Viktor Orban frequently in conflict with other EU partners. Polish prime minister Donald Tusk is expected to oversee a return to consensus-building. Warsaw says it will prioritize security and defence policies during its six month stint.
